title: server_failure
description: A production server fails unexpectedly, taking down hosted services.

timeline.title: Monitoring Alert Triggered
timeline.description: Monitoring systems detect the production server has stopped responding to health checks.

timeline.title: Services Go Offline
timeline.description: Web applications and APIs hosted on the server become unreachable. Users begin reporting outages.

timeline.title: On-Call Engineer Paged
timeline.description: The on-call engineer receives a PagerDuty alert and begins initial triage.

timeline.title: Root Cause Identified
timeline.description: Investigation reveals a hardware fault caused a kernel panic and server halt.

timeline.title: Failover Initiated
timeline.description: Traffic is redirected to standby servers while the failed host is examined.

timeline.title: Services Restored
timeline.description: All services are restored on standby infrastructure. Post-incident review is scheduled.

node-1.title: Production Server Unresponsive
node-1.situation: You receive a critical alert: a production server is not responding to health checks and all hosted services are down. Users are actively impacted. You have remote access to the management console.
node-1.question: What is your immediate action?

options.label: A
options.text: Acknowledge the alert, notify stakeholders of the outage, and begin triage by checking the server console for error messages.

options.label: B
options.text: Immediately reboot the server without investigating the cause to restore services as fast as possible.

options.label: C
options.text: Wait 15 minutes to see if the server recovers on its own before taking action.

node-2.title: Console Diagnostics
node-2.situation: The server console shows a kernel panic caused by a hardware fault on the primary NIC. Monitoring confirms no network packets have been received for 12 minutes. A warm standby server is available.
node-2.question: How do you proceed?

options.label: A
options.text: Initiate failover by redirecting DNS and load-balancer traffic to the standby server while preserving the failed server for forensic analysis.

options.label: B
options.text: Attempt to hot-swap the NIC in the running server without powering it down.

node-3.title: Failover Execution
node-3.situation: Traffic has been redirected to the standby server and services are recovering. You must now validate service health and ensure the standby can sustain production load.
node-3.question: What do you do?

options.label: A
options.text: Run automated smoke tests against the standby, monitor CPU and memory metrics, and confirm all dependent services are operational.

options.label: B
options.text: Declare the incident resolved immediately after traffic redirection without verifying service health.

node-4.title: Post-Failover Stabilization
node-4.situation: The standby server is handling production traffic. The failed server has been isolated. Management asks for an estimated time to full resolution.
node-4.question: What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Document the incident timeline, coordinate hardware replacement for the failed server, and schedule a post-incident review within 48 hours.

options.label: B
options.text: Bring the failed server back online immediately without replacing the faulty hardware to reduce costs.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: You successfully identified the hardware fault, executed a clean failover to the standby server, validated service health, and initiated the post-incident review process. Production services are fully restored.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
